# [Colorado State University](https://www.colostate.edu)

![Colorado State University](https://static.colostate.edu/logo/reslogo-v2/assets/img/csu-responsive-symbol.min.svg)

## [AgNext](https://agnext.colostate.edu)

# Ruminants as Part of a Sustainable Agriculture System

By: Samantha Clark, Logan Thompson Ph.D., and Kim Stackhouse-Lawson Ph.D.

Every person needs food, and should have access to a safe, nutritious, and affordable diet. All food production produces greenhouse gases, whether plant or animal sourced, and humanity depends on agriculture for food production. Livestock have an important role in the economy, culture, ecosystems, health, and more. Agriculture, including livestock production, has been an important sector of the U.S. economy and is vital to the success of rural areas. A successful agriculture sector supports economic growth overall, as agriculture provides nutritious food across the country.

Ruminants are upcyclers that can utilize marginal land and can convert human-inedible plant proteins, human food leftovers, and byproducts into high-quality protein, rich in micronutrients (Place, 2017; Fessenden, 2020). They can graze land that is unsuitable for other uses, allowing for profit, and food production in rural areas. Grazing land can provide multiple environmental benefits to the soil, biodiversity and wildlife, and reductions in global greenhouse gas emissions (Varijakshapanicker et al., 2019). Grazing ruminants can promote habitat for wildlife species, and help preserve water sources. Managed livestock systems can play a large role in carbon management. Grazing lands can sequester carbon from the atmosphere, acting as a carbon sink, and/or store and protect carbon already present in the soil (Sollenberger et al., 2019). Ruminants also play an important role in the biogenic carbon cycle (Thompson and Rowntree, 2020). Plants capture atmospheric carbon through photosynthesis, and ruminants are able to convert that carbon into usable end products. Ruminants respirate and excrete part of the carbon they ingest back into the atmosphere, where it can be broken back down into carbon dioxide or incorporated into soil for utilization.

Humans need carbohydrates, protein, fats, essential amino acids, vitamins, and minerals (Varijakshapanicker et al., 2019). Animal-sourced foods contain sources of energy, protein, minerals, and vitamins, and provide a balanced profile of amino acids. They provide many of the nutrients that are lacking or less bioavailable in plant-based foods. Animal-sourced foods are the best nutrient-dense foods for children ages 6-23 months (Adesogan et al., 2019). Intake of animal-sourced foods improves growth, physical activity, and leads to better pregnancy outcomes and reduced morbidity from illness (Adesogan et al., 2019). People in low- and middle-income countries have improved health outcomes when provided low to medium levels of ruminant products (Adesogan et al., 2019). If animal-sourced foods were removed from U.S. diets, micronutrient deficiencies would prevail (White and Hall, 2018).

It is important to also consider that livestock animals contribute to multiple different products, not just human food. They are also important for production of leather products, pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, pet food, and so much more (Banda and Tanganyika, 2021). Livestock production is also needed for wax paper, crayons, paints, rubber, lubricants, candles, soaps, lipsticks, shaving creams, and other cosmetics. Animal manure is also commonly used as natural fertilizer, as insect repellant and is increasingly being utilized to produce bioenergy. Livestock can be utilized in weed control using targeted grazing, and to clear crop residues after harvest. Some ruminants, especially sheep, also provide fiber that can be used for clothing, insulation, and bedding.

Bottom Line: Livestock are an important part of Food Systems, and necessary for sustainability.

**Sources:**

Adesogan, A. T., A. H. Havelaar, S. L. McKune, M. Eilittä, and G. E. Dahl. 2019. Animal source foods: Sustainability problem or malnutrition and sustainability solution? Perspective matters. Glob. Food Secur. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gfs.2019.100325

Banda, L. J. and J. Tanganyika. 2021. Livestock provide more than food in smallholder production systems of developing countries. Animal Frontiers. 11:7-14. https://doi.org/10.1093/af/vfab001

Fessenden, M. 2020. Cattle Upcycle Waste Food into Quality Protein. Agricultural Modeling and Training Systems, LLC. Available from: https://agmodelsystems.com/cattle-upcycle-waste-food-into-quality-protein/

Kebreab, E. 2013. Sustainable Animal Agriculture. CABI.

Klobuchar, A. 2013. The Economic Contribution of America’s Farmers and The Importance of Agricultural Exports. Joint Economic Committee. Available from: https://www.jec.senate.gov/public/\_cache/files/266a0bf3-5142-4545-b806-ef9fd78b9c2f/jec-agriculture-report.pdf

Place, S. 2017. FAO Affirms Cattle’s Critical Role as Upcycler. Medium. Available from: https://medium.com/@beefitsfordinner/fao-affirms-cattles-critical-role-as-upcycler-11fd32a3e85

Sollenberger, L. E., M. M. Kohmann, J. C. B. Dubeux, and M. L. Silveira. 2019. Grassland Management Affects Delivery of Regulating and Supporting Ecosystem Services. Crop Sci. 59:441-459.

Thompson, L., and Rowntree, J., 2020. Invited Review: Methane sources, quantification, and mitigation in grazing beef systems. Appl Ani Sci. 36:556-573. https://doi.org/10.15232/aas.2019-01951

Varijakshapanicker, P., S. Mckune, L. Miller, S. Hendrickx, M. Balehegn, G. E. Dahl, and A. T. Adesogan. 2019. Sustainable livestock systems to improve human health, nutrition, and economic status. Animal Frontiers. 9:39-50. https://doi.org/10.1093/af/vfz041

White, R., and M. Hall. 2018. Environmental and nutritional implications of agriculture without animals. J Ani Sci. https://www.proquest.com/docview/2166302887?pq-origsite=gscholar&fromopenview=true

--- SOURCE 4: Sustainable Livestock Farming Practices for Resilience ---
URL: https://alliancebioversityciat.org/stories/sustainable-livestock-farming-practices-resilience

SUMMARY:
However, it is increasingly recognized that modern livestock farming practices – often involving mass production and intense resource use – contribute to climate change environmental degradation.However, alternatives exist, and plenty research indicates that a combination of improved livestock feed and integrated livestock farming methods could reduce the impact of livestock farming and even offer important ecosystem benefits such as the improvement of soil fertility. Adopting and mainstreaming these practices offer the opportunity to address the increasing global demand for animal products whilst reducing the environmental impacts of industrial production, using livestock as an integral part of more sustainable farming systems that balance demand with productivity, climate change action, environmental stewardship, the protection of farmers’ livelihoods and economic viability. Livestock farming practices such as rotational grazing, agroforestry, silvopastoral systems, precision agriculture, increased animal welfare and adapted forages to reduce methane emissions are essential.

**Blog**Sustainable Livestock Farming Practices for Resilience
==============================================================

![Image 2: Sustainable Livestock Farming Practices for Resilience](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/sites/default/files/styles/1920_scale/public/sustainable-livestock-farming-practices-for-resilience_0.jpg?itok=44h63c64)

on October 11, 2024
*   [Agroforestry](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/tag/agroforestry) 
*   [Climate action](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/research-themes/climate-action) 
*   [Climate change mitigation](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/tag/climate-change-mitigation) 
*   [Crops for nutrition and health](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/research-themes/crops-nutrition-and-health) 
*   [Multifunctional landscapes](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/research-themes/multifunctional-landscapes) 
*   [Regenerative agriculture](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/tag/regenerative-agriculture) 
*   [Soil health](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/tag/soil-health) 
*   [Sustainable livestock](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/tag/sustainable-livestock) 
*   [Tropical Forages](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/crops/tropical-forages) 

Livestock farming is a cornerstone of agriculture globally, providing essential food products, livelihoods, and increasing opportunities for innovation. However, it is increasingly recognized that modern livestock farming practices – often involving mass production and intense resource use – [contribute to climate change environmental degradation](https://www.fao.org/4/x5305e/x5305e02.htm#:~:text=Livestock%20in%20a%20changing%20world,-The%20association%20between&text=Large%20land%20areas%20have%20become,areas%20is%20affected%20by%20livestock.).However, alternatives exist, and plenty research indicates that a combination of improved livestock feed and integrated livestock farming methods could [reduce the impact of livestock farming and even offer important ecosystem benefits such as the improvement of soil fertility.](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/gfs.12592)

Adopting and mainstreaming these practices offer the opportunity to address the increasing global demand for animal products whilst reducing the environmental impacts of industrial production, using livestock as an integral part of more sustainable farming systems that balance demand with productivity, climate change action, environmental stewardship, the protection of farmers’ livelihoods and economic viability.

In this article we will explore key sustainable livestock farming practices that contribute to resilience, ensuring that farming innovations allow agriculture to not only withstand challenges such as climate change, economic pressures, and resource constraints, but to positively contribute to facing these challenges.

![Image 3](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/sites/default/files/styles/1920_scale/public/sustainable-livestock-farming-practices-for-resilience.jpg?itok=APrJCUdO)

A farmer in his silvopastoral grazing system in Colombia. Credit: CIAT/Neil Palmer

What is sustainable livestock farming?
--------------------------------------

In contrast to today’s conventional methods of livestock farming, sustainable livestock farming integrates environmental, social, and economic factors into animal production systems, thus minimizing negative impacts on ecosystems, conserving natural resources and increasing animal welfare. Sustainable livestock farming often revolves around the following methods that reduce pollution, conserve water, and minimize the use of chemical agricultural inputs:

![Image 4: Sustainable Livestock Farming Practices for Resilience - Image 4](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/sites/default/files/styles/1920_scale/public/images/sustainable-livestock-farming-practices-for-resilience-image-4.jpg?itok=Fu-Nf1qg)

Cows graze freely on a diverse natural landscape in Kenya. Credit: CIAT/Neil Palmer

Rotational grazing
------------------

**[Rotational grazing](https://rodaleinstitute.org/why-organic/organic-farming-practices/rotational-grazing/)** is a method of livestock pasture management that mimics the natural grazing patterns of wild herbivores. Instead of allowing livestock to graze continuously on the same land or feeding them with alternative feed sources, farmers following the rotational grazing method divide pastures into smaller sections (known as paddocks), and graze their animals one paddock at a time, leaving the other sections to ‘rest’, during which the forages regenerate and deepen their roots. Rotational grazing has numerous benefits, including:

*   **Improved soil health:**[Rotational grazing encourages deep root growth in the land paddocks that are resting, which enhances soil structure and water retention.](https://sandcountyfoundation.org/news/2023/soil-health-rotational-grazing#:~:text=Rotational%20livestock%20grazing%20allows%20plants,supports%20bird%20and%20wildlife%20habitat.) By alternating rest with grazing, this method reduces soil erosion and increases the soil’s ability to absorb rainwater, making the pasture more resilient to drought whilst also fertilizing the land with the animals’ manure.

*   **Increased biodiversity:** As different areas of the pasture are allowed to recover between grazing periods, [plant diversity increases,](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167880917300932) which supports a wider range of wildlife and insect species.

*   **Reduced need for fertilizers:** Rotational grazing naturally recycles nutrients as livestock manure is distributed more evenly across the paddocks. [This reduces the need for synthetic fertilizers](https://www.farmprogress.com/pasture/minimizing-inputs-with-300-days-of-rotational-grazing-), which have harmful environmental impacts such as degrading soil and polluting nearby waterways.

*   **Enhanced pasture productivity:** Well-managed rotational grazing can [improve pasture productivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0167880922002249), resulting in higher forage quality for livestock, leading to healthier animals and better yields of meat, milk, or wool.

For farmers, rotational grazing is a cost-effective way to improve the resilience of their land and livestock. By preventing overgrazing, this practice ensures that pastures remain productive in the long term.

Agroforestry integration:
-------------------------

**Agroforestry** is the practice of integrating trees and shrubs into agricultural systems, including livestock farming. Known as [silvopastoral systems](https://www.agroforestry.ac.uk/agroforestry-systems/pastoral), land that integrates trees, forage plants and livestock provide multiple environmental and economic benefits, including:

*   **Improved microclimate:**[Trees provide shade and shelter](https://www.fao.org/nr/sustainability/foro-electronico/proyectos/projects-detail/es/c/216258/) for livestock, protecting them from extreme weather conditions such as heat and cold. This reduces heat stress in animals, [improving their welfare and productivity](https://www.fao.org/nr/sustainability/foro-electronico/proyectos/projects-detail/es/c/216258/).

*   **Carbon sequestration:**[Trees capture and store carbon dioxide, helping to mitigate climate change](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/stories/agrobiodiversity-climate-action-ecosystem-restoration). Agroforestry systems act as carbon sinks, absorbing more carbon than traditional open pastures. According to [a study by Nature, silvopastoral systems stored 27–163% more carbon compared to open pasturelands.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-21089-4#:~:text=Silvopastoral%20systems%20and%20forest%20remnants,land%2Dbased%20carbon%20mitigation%20strategy.)

*   **Soil improvement:**[Tree roots help stabilize the soil and prevent erosion.](https://ourlandandwater.nz/news/benefits-of-trees-in-pasture-go-beyond-erosion-control/) The leaf litter from trees enriches the soil with organic matter, improving fertility and water retention.

*   **Biodiversity:**[The integration of trees into livestock pasture increases biodiversity](https://efi.int/sites/default/files/files/publication-bank/2018/tr_21.pdf) by creating habitats for birds, insects, and other wildlife. This enhances the overall health of the ecosystem and can provide natural pest control.

*   **Diversification of income:**Farmers can harvest timber, fruit, nuts, or other products from trees, [providing an additional source of income](https://openknowledge.fao.org/server/api/core/bitstreams/f78403a3-d9c2-4b81-b999-85031935875c/content) alongside livestock production.

Agroforestry systems are particularly effective in tropical and subtropical regions, where trees can thrive and provide year-round benefits. However, they are also being adopted in temperate regions as part of broader efforts to increase farm resilience and sustainability, with positive results. [Discover Alliance women-led initiatives to integrate trees and livestock in multiple countries here](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/stories/women-lead-sustainable-livestock-farming-near-amazon-rainforest-colombia).

![Image 5: Sustainable Livestock Farming Practices for Resilience - Image 5](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/sites/default/files/styles/1920_scale/public/images/sustainable-livestock-farming-practices-for-resilience-image-5.jpg?itok=3uxSZV7Q)

A farmer in Vietnam harvests livestock forages to feed to his cattle. Credit: CIAT/Georgina Smith

Reduced methane emissions
-------------------------

Livestock farming, particularly the production of ruminants such as cows and sheep, is responsible for significant methane emissions. **Methane** is a greenhouse gas that is far more potent than carbon dioxide, making it a critical target for climate mitigation efforts. However, **methane emissions** can be reduced through a variety of methods, including:

*   **Improved diets:**The type of feed given to livestock can have a significant impact on the amount of methane they produce during digestion. High-quality [forages](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/crops/tropical-forages), grains, and feed additives such as oils or seaweed can reduce methane emissions. For example, [adding red seaweed (asparagopsis) to the diet of ruminants has been shown to reduce methane production by up to 80%](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7968649/). [The Alliance does extensive work globally to improve and support the adoption of optimal forage varieties](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/crops/tropical-forages), which can be [explored here](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/crops/tropical-forages).

*   **Genetic selection:** Selective breeding programs are being developed to breed livestock that produce lower methane emissions per unit of production. By choosing animals with better feed efficiency and lower methane output, farmers can reduce the overall environmental impact of their herds.

*   **Manure management:** Manure is another significant source of methane emissions, particularly when stored in anaerobic conditions. Implementing better manure management practices, such as composting or using anaerobic digesters to produce biogas, [can significantly reduce methane emissions](https://www.epa.gov/agstar/practices-reduce-methane-emissions-livestock-manure-management). [Biogas systems capture methane from manure and convert it into renewable energy,](https://www.eesi.org/papers/view/fact-sheet-biogasconverting-waste-to-energy) which can be used to power farm operations or sold to the grid.

By adopting these strategies, farmers can play a critical role in mitigating climate change while improving the efficiency and productivity of their livestock farming.

Improved water management
-------------------------

Water is a critical resource in livestock farming, and its conservation is essential for long-term production. Livestock require significant amounts of water for drinking, cleaning, and cooling, while pastures and crops also depend on water for growth. **Sustainable water management practices**aim to reduce water consumption, protect water quality, and improve the efficiency of water use on farms. Key water conservation practices include:

*   **Efficient irrigation systems:** Traditional flood irrigation systems are often wasteful, leading to significant water loss through evaporation and runoff. Sustainable farms are increasingly adopting more [efficient irrigation methods, such as drip or sprinkler systems, which deliver water directly to the roots of plants.](https://www.twl-irrigation.com/the-most-and-least-efficient-methods-of-irrigation/) These systems reduce water waste and ensure that crops and pastures receive the precise amount of water they need.

*   **Rainwater harvesting:**Capturing and storing rainwater for use during dry periods can help [reduce reliance on external water sources.](https://smartwateronline.com/news/rainwater-as-a-solution-to-water-scarcity-case-studies-from-around-the-world?srsltid=AfmBOoqDN3mFnoKcmWv5mxXZlr1drBqPBL6AWlqYVf8zCih1N1mkpF_u) Rainwater can be collected from the roofs of buildings or specially designed catchment systems and stored in tanks for later use in watering livestock or irrigating pastures.

*   **Riparian buffer zones:**[Planting vegetation along the edges of streams and rivers can help filter runoff from pastures and prevent pollutants such as manure, fertilizers, and pesticides from entering water bodies](https://www.teagasc.ie/environment/water-quality/farming-for-water-quality-assap/assap-factsheets/riparian-buffer-zones/#:~:text=What%20is%20a%20Riparian%20Buffer,of%20nutrients%2C%20sediment%20and%20chemicals.). These buffer zones also provide habitats for wildlife and contribute to biodiversity.

*   **Water recycling:**Implementing systems that recycle water used for cleaning and cooling livestock facilities can significantly reduce overall water consumption. For example, [water used to clean milking equipment can be filtered and reused for irrigation or livestock drinking.](https://medium.com/water-food-nexus/water-recycling-and-reuse-in-agriculture-for-circularity-of-food-and-water-f08fe4b131b3)

Sustainable water management is especially important in regions facing water scarcity or where competition for water resources is intense. By adopting these practices, livestock farmers can reduce their water footprint and ensure the long-term sustainability of their operations.

![Image 6: Sustainable Livestock Farming Practices for Resilience - Image 3](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/sites/default/files/styles/1920_scale/public/images/sustainable-livestock-farming-practices-for-resilience-image-3.jpg?itok=sQhxSBUc)

A cow rests with a bird on its back, showing animal diversity in open grazing systems. Credit: CIAT/Juan Pablo Marin García

Integrated pest management
--------------------------

**Integrated Pest Management (IPM)** is a holistic approach to pest control that minimizes the use of chemical pesticides and focuses on long-term pest prevention and ecosystem health. IPM combines biological, cultural, mechanical and chemical control methods to manage pests in a way that is economically viable and environmentally sustainable. In livestock farming, pests such as flies, ticks, and worms can cause significant problems for animal health, and IPM strategies aim to control these pests while minimizing harm to the environment. Common IPM techniques in livestock farming include:

*   **Biological control:**This involves [introducing natural predators, parasites, or pathogens to control pest populations](https://lgpress.clemson.edu/publication/biological-control-strategies-in-integrated-pest-management-ipm-programs/). For example, dung beetles can help break down livestock manure, reducing the breeding grounds for flies.

*   **Cultural practices:** Rotating pastures, maintaining clean and dry housing, and promoting healthy soil can reduce pest problems. For example, [rotating pastures disrupts the life cycle of parasites, reducing their impact on livestock.](https://www.vet.cornell.edu/animal-health-diagnostic-center/programs/nyschap/modules-documents/best-management-practices-pasture-parasite-management)

*   **Mechanical controls:** Physical barriers, traps, or manual removal can be effective in controlling pests. For instance, [fly traps can be used around barns](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0022030218301747), while mechanical brushes can help remove ticks from livestock.

IPM not only reduces the need for harmful chemical inputs but also promotes biodiversity, improves animal welfare and supports the overall health of the farming ecosystem.

Animal welfare and its role in sustainability
---------------------------------------------

**Animal welfare** is a critical component of sustainable livestock farming. Ensuring that animals are raised in humane conditions not only aligns with ethical standards but also contributes to farm productivity and resilience. Key practices to improve animal welfare in sustainable livestock farming include:

*   **Free-range and pasture-based systems:** Allowing animals to [roam freely in natural environments](https://sentientmedia.org/free-range/) supports their physical and psychological well-being. It also [enhances the nutritional and flavor quality of meat, milk, and eggs](https://growingtogive.org/blog-posts-page-twenty-nine.html).

*   **Increased health through stress reduction:** Minimizing animal stress through proper housing, handling, and nutrition improves immune function and [reduces the need for antibiotics](https://epha.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/02/report-ending-routine-farm-antibiotic-use-in-europe-final-2022.pdf).

*   **Natural behaviors:** Designing farming systems [that enable animals to express natural behaviors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0168159106001067), such as rooting, pecking, or grazing, [promotes healthier and more content livestock](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0168159106001067).

Investing in animal welfare leads to better long-term productivity and consumer trust, as more people become concerned about the ethical aspects of food production. In addition to ethical considerations, improving animal welfare has several practical benefits for livestock farmers: Increasing consumer interest in more ethical practices also offers income opportunities for farmers who prioritize animal welfare.

![Image 7: Sustainable Livestock Farming Practices for Resilience - Image 6](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/sites/default/files/styles/1920_scale/public/images/sustainable-livestock-farming-practices-for-resilience-image-6.jpg?itok=GU90-cjo)

A farmer celebrates his harvest of improved livestock forages for his silvopastoral system in Colombia. Credit: CIAT/Neil Palmer

Optimal use of technology in sustainable livestock farming
----------------------------------------------------------

**Technological innovations** are playing an increasingly important role in increasing the sustainability of farming, including livestock farming practices. Technological advancements can allow farmers to monitor their animals’ wellbeing, improve resource efficiency and reduce environmental impact. Important technological advances for livestock farming include:

*   **Precision livestock farming:**Precision farming in the context of livestock refers to the use of [sensors, data analytics, and automation to monitor and manage livestock more effectively](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/veterinary-science-and-veterinary-medicine/precision-livestock-farming). These technological tools can track animal health, behavior and productivity in real time, allowing farmers to make data-driven decisions. Precision livestock farming technologies include:

*   **Wearable sensors:**[These devices monitor vital signs such as heart rate, body temperature, and activity levels in livestock.](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/22/12/4319) Early detection of illness or stress allows farmers to intervene before problems escalate, reducing the need for antibiotics and improving animal welfare. Discover how the Alliance is using [wearable GPS systems to track animal behavior and increase welfare here](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/stories/moving-forward-sustainable-forage-based-livestock-production-systems-colombia).

*   **Automated feeding systems:**These systems [use sensors to monitor individual animals' nutritional needs and deliver the right amount of feed at the right time](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/287295959_Precision_livestock_farming_Precision_feeding_technologies_and_sustainable_livestock_production). This reduces waste, optimizes feed efficiency, and ensures that animals receive the nutrition they need.

*   **Pasture monitoring:**[Drones or satellite imagery can be used to monitor the health and productivity of pastures](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8388655/), allowing farmers to optimize grazing patterns and prevent overgrazing.

*   **Genetic advances:** Advances in genetics are enabling [farmers to breed animals with traits that enhance sustainability](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/cmsdata/239025/Animal%20breeders%202%20-%20what%20is%20animal%20breeding-%20why%20is%20it%20important-%20EFFAB.pdf). Selective breeding can reduce methane emissions, improve feed efficiency, and increase disease resistance. This reduces the need for antibiotics and other inputs while improving overall farm productivity. In some cases, genetic modification is being explored to produce livestock that are more resilient to environmental stressors such as heat or drought. While genetic modification remains a controversial topic, it has the potential to enhance the sustainability of livestock farming, especially in regions prone to extreme weather conditions.

*   **Biogas Systems:**[These systems capture methane from manure and convert it into renewable energy.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S240584402410535X) Not only do biogas systems reduce methane emissions, but they also provide a source of renewable energy that can be used to power farm equipment or heat buildings. By incorporating renewable energy into their operations, livestock farmers can reduce their carbon footprint and contribute to the global transition to clean energy.

![Image 8: Sustainable Livestock Farming Practices for Resilience - Image  7](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/sites/default/files/styles/1920_scale/public/images/sustainable-livestock-farming-practices-for-resilience-image-7.jpg?itok=HfaD-52V)

A woman feeds improved forages to her cows in Vietnam, following a training on integrated soil fertility management. Credit: CIAT/Georgina Smith

![Image 9: Sustainable Livestock Farming Practices for Resilience - Image  8](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/sites/default/files/styles/1920_scale/public/images/sustainable-livestock-farming-practices-for-resilience-image-8.jpg?itok=sTNG0FZx)

A herd of cows graze and rest amongst trees in Eastern Colombia. Credit: CIAT/Neil Palmer

Challenges and barriers to the adoption of sustainable livestock farming practices
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

While sustainable livestock farming practices offer numerous benefits, several challenges can hinder their widespread adoption. These include:

*   **Initial costs:** Many sustainable practices – such as installing biogas systems or adopting precision farming technology – require significant upfront investment. With the future rewards still unknown, these initial costs may block many farmers from joining the sustainable livestock farming movement.

*   **Knowledge and training:** Farmers may lack the technical knowledge or access to training needed to implement sustainable practices effectively, meaning that awareness of the practices laid out above does not reach all livestock farmers.

*   **Market access:** Sustainable products often require premium markets, and many farmers may struggle to find buyers willing to pay higher prices for sustainably produced meat, milk, or eggs.

*   **Policy support:** In many regions, agricultural policies do not provide sufficient incentives or support for farmers to transition to sustainable practices.

Despite these challenges, some governments, NGOs and private sector actors are offering increasing support in the form of grants, technical assistance and market opportunities to encourage the adoption of sustainable livestock farming practices.

![Image 10: Sustainable Livestock Farming Practices for Resilience - Image  9](https://alliancebioversityciat.org/sites/default/files/styles/1920_scale/public/images/sustainable-livestock-farming-practices-for-resilience-image-9.jpg?itok=K0jd2p7_)

A farmer transports cattle in Cambodia. Credit: CIAT/Georgina Smith

The future of sustainable livestock farming
-------------------------------------------

# Role of ruminant livestock in sustainable agricultural systems

### Affiliation

# Role of ruminant livestock in sustainable agricultural systems

### Authors

### Affiliation

## Abstract

Ruminants have served and will continue to serve a valuable role in sustainable agricultural systems. They are particularly useful in converting vast renewable resources from rangeland, pasture, and crop residues or other by-products into food edible for humans. With ruminants, land that is too poor or too erodable to cultivate becomes productive. Also, nutrients in by-products are utilized and do not become a waste-disposal problem. The need to maintain ruminants to utilize these humanly inedible foodstuffs and convert them into high-quality foods for human consumption has been a characteristic of advanced societies for several thousand years. Further, ruminant livestock production is entirely consistent with proper agronomy practices in which forages are grown on 25% of arable land to minimize water and soil erosion. Questions have been asked, however, about the use of humanly edible foodstuffs (grains, protein sources, etc.) in ruminant diets. Does their use create a net loss of nutrients for human consumption? What level of their use is necessary or desirable? Does the use of some of these improve the nutrient (e.g. protein) quality or product value? Too often the opponents of animal agriculture evaluate the desirability of animal production on gross calorie or protein intake/output values. However, in many cases the feeds used in animal production are not consumable by humans, and in order to properly evaluate animal production, humanly consumable energy and protein intake should be used for efficiency comparisons. Analysis of the costs/returns of humanly edible energy and protein for a variety of dairy and beef cattle production systems shows that food value is increased with ruminant products, and that net returns of humanly edible nutrients are dependent on the production system used. The efficiency with which ruminants convert humanly edible energy and protein into meat or milk is highly dependent on diet, and hence, on regional production practices. Previous studies suggest that in the United States, dairy production efficiency ranges from 96 to 276% on a humanly consumable protein basis. Beef production efficiency is very dependent on the time spent in the feedlot and digestible energy and protein efficiencies range from 28 to 59% and 52 to 104%, respectively. However, beef production can add to the humanly consumable protein pool depending on the feeding schedule. In addition, the protein resulting from ruminant livestock production is of higher quality with a greater biological value than protein in the substrate feeds. The evidence that ruminant livestock belong in sustainable livestock production systems is convincing.

enriched_points.point_title: Balancing food production and ecological health
enriched_points.expanded_content: Integrating ruminants into sustainable agriculture enhances food security while promoting biodiversity, soil health, and carbon sequestration, addressing both nutritional needs and environmental preservation.

Implementing sustainable livestock practices, such as rotational grazing and agroforestry, mitigates greenhouse gas emissions, improves soil fertility, and supports ecosystem resilience, ensuring long-term agricultural viability.

Innovative approaches, including precision livestock farming and genetic improvements, will enhance ruminant efficiency, reduce environmental impacts, and secure livelihoods in a changing climate, fostering sustainable agriculture.
